planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or early fall by splitting the root mass with two forks back-to-back; citron daylily establishes quickly and multiplies steadily. Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade. Being an evening-blooming species, site it where it can be enjoyed from patios or windows in the evening hours when the fragrance is most apparent.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; citron daylily is a Chinese species daylily that differs from the common hybrid daylilies in two remarkable ways — it blooms in the evening and at night (opening after 5 pm and lasting through the following morning before closing) and its flowers are strongly fragrant, with a sweet, lemony scent that carries through the garden on summer evenings. The flowers are pale, clear, lemon-yellow — more delicate and refined in color than most hybrid daylilies — and appear from July onward, later than most daylilies and filling the summer sequence. The plants form large, arching clumps of strap-like foliage reaching 3–4 feet, with the slender flower scapes rising well above the leaves and bearing multiple buds that open successively over an extended period. As a parent of many early daylily hybrids, it contributed both fragrance and late-season bloom genetics to the daylily breeding program. For evening garden use — gardens intended to be enjoyed after work hours — it is uniquely valuable for fragrance after sunset when hybrid daylilies have closed.

planting.note: Divide spreading clumps in spring or fall; lemon daylily spreads readily by underground rhizomes to form extensive colonies and may become too vigorous for small spaces. In rich, moist soils it spreads more aggressively. Plant in moist, fertile soil in full sun to part shade. This ancient species has been cultivated in European gardens since the 1500s.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; lemon daylily is one of the oldest ornamental plants in European garden history — a Chinese native introduced to European gardens in the late 1500s that appears in botanical illustrations from the 1600s and has grown in cottage gardens and farmsteads continuously ever since. The flowers are clear, clean, bright-yellow and strongly sweet-fragrant, carried on arching stems above fine-textured, narrow foliage in May and June — among the earliest daylilies to bloom. The fragrance is a classic, fresh, sweet lily-like scent that carries well in still evening air. Unlike most hybrid daylilies (which are crosses from several species including this one), lemon daylily is a true species that spreads modestly by rhizomes to form naturalized drifts and can be found half-wild in old rural gardens across temperate regions. The earliest-blooming daylily species in temperate gardens, filling the gap before the early hybrids begin their season. Buds and young flowers are edible as a vegetable in Chinese cooking.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in part to full shade; climbing hydrangea vine is a streamside and swamp-forest plant in nature and requires consistent soil moisture. It climbs by aerial rootlets and establishes well on tree trunks, rough stone, and brick without additional support.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in part to full shade; climbing hydrangea vine is a native eastern North American alternative to the Asian climbing hydrangea (H. anomala subsp. petiolaris), found naturally in moist forests and swamp edges from Virginia to Florida and Louisiana. It climbs trees, walls, and structures by clinging aerial rootlets, and in late spring produces clusters of small, sweet-fragrant, white flowers without the large sterile bracts of the Asian species — more modest but very sweetly scented and genuinely attractive to pollinators. The leaves are glossy, dark-green, and semi-evergreen to evergreen in zones 7–9, making it useful for year-round coverage of shaded surfaces. It tolerates deeper shade than most vines and is one of the few self-clinging climbers native to the southeastern US. Outstanding for climbing shaded tree trunks, stone walls, and other structures in moist, shaded southern gardens where Asian vines perform poorly. Supports the ecology of the forest system it comes from — its berries are eaten by several bird species.

planting.note: Scarify seeds or soak overnight before sowing in moist, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Carolina bush pea is a legume that fixes nitrogen and improves poor soil. It develops a deep taproot and resents transplanting once established — site carefully. Plants are very long-lived and clumps expand slowly over many years.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in well-drained, average to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Carolina bush pea is a tall, upright native legume of the southern Appalachians producing striking, dense spikes of bright yellow pea flowers in May and June that strongly resemble lupines — it is one of the few native alternatives to lupines for the middle South where lupines fail in summer heat. The clover-like, three-parted leaves are attractive through the season and the whole plant, including the hairy leaves and stems, has a pleasingly robust, textural quality. After flowering, the inflated seed pods are ornamental. As a nitrogen-fixing legume it thrives in lean, infertile soil where other ornamentals struggle. It is extremely long-lived — plants may persist for 25 or more years without division or disturbance — and forms impressive clumps of increasing magnificence. The common name 'Aaron's Rod' refers to the tall, upright flowering spikes. Excellent as a lupine substitute for gardens in the mid-South where genuine lupines perform poorly in summer heat.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring; Formosa lily is remarkably fast from seed — flowering in as little as 90–120 days — making it one of the fastest seed-to-flower lilies available. In mild climates (zone 7+) it naturalizes as a short-lived perennial or self-sowing biennial. It is susceptible to lily mosaic virus carried by aphids; protect with aphid control or grow from fresh, disease-free seed each season.

directions: Sow seeds in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Formosa lily is a tall, slender, fragrant lily native to Taiwan that blooms in August through October — far later than most lilies — producing large (4–6 inch), pure-white, trumpet-shaped flowers with a rich, sweet fragrance on the exterior, with a yellow center fading to purple-streaked markings on the inside of the tube. It is one of the most fragrant lilies grown in gardens and one of the latest-blooming, providing essential late-season fragrance and large-scale floral impact when most lilies have long finished. Remarkably, it blooms from seed in 90–120 days — faster than almost any other lily — and self-sows readily in mild climates to maintain a permanent, naturalized colony. It is ideal for Gulf Coast and zone 8–10 gardens where summer heat and humidity defeat many bulb lilies. In zone 6–7 it performs as an annually-replanted bulb. The pure-white fall flowers are striking against the late summer garden.
